title: Listening - Part 3: The Plastic Brain
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.
instructions: You will hear an interview with two experts on the brain, neuroscientist David Vance and educator Claire Holt, about the concept of neuroplasticity. For questions 15 – 20, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which fits best according to what you hear.

extracts.context: An interview discussing how the brain changes throughout life and its implications for modern education.
extracts.audio_script:
Interviewer: Today we’re exploring the fascinating world of neuroplasticity with Dr. David Vance and Claire Holt. David, we used to believe the brain was 'fixed' after childhood. How wrong were we?

David: Spectacularly wrong. [15]The discovery that the brain's physical structure continues to reorganize itself in response to new experiences well into old age is the most profound shift in neuroscience.[/15] It means we aren't just stuck with the hardware we were born with; we are constantly rewiring ourselves. It’s an incredibly hopeful message.

Interviewer: Claire, as an educator, how has this changed your approach in the classroom?

Claire: It has completely debunked the 'I’m just not a math person' myth. [16]What I tell my students now is that struggle isn't a sign of failure, it’s actually the sound of the brain growing new connections.[/16] If we can get children to see their intelligence as a muscle that they can build, rather than a fixed bucket that gets filled, their motivation sky-rockets. It’s about teaching 'growth mindset' through the lens of biology.

Interviewer: David, you’ve done studies on London taxi drivers. What did that reveal?

David: Ah, the famous 'Knowledge' study. [17]We found that the part of the brain responsible for spatial navigation actually grew in volume as the drivers memorized the city streets.[/17] It was a clear demonstration of 'use it or lose it'. However, what was even more interesting was that once they retired and stopped driving, that area began to shrink back to its original size. The brain is efficient—it won't maintain a highway it doesn't use.

Interviewer: Claire, some people say that technology is 'rotting' our brains. Does the science support that?

Claire: 'Rotting' is a strong word. But [18]I am concerned that our reliance on instant digital answers is weakening our capacity for deep, sustained focus.[/18] If the brain is plastic, it will adapt to the environment we give it. If that environment is a series of thirty-second clips, the brain will become very good at processing short bursts of info but might lose the 'muscle' for reading a complex novel.

Interviewer: Is there an age limit to this plasticity, David?

David: Not really, though it does slow down. [19]I was actually quite moved by a recent study of seventy-year-olds who learned to play the piano and showed significant increases in cognitive flexibility.[/19] It’s never too late to start a new hobby. The challenge for older adults isn't that their brains can't change, it's that they often stop challenging them in new ways.

Interviewer: Finally, what is the best thing we can do for our brains today?

Claire: [20]I would say seek out something that makes you feel like a beginner again.[/20] That feeling of being slightly out of your depth is exactly where the growth happens. David always tells me that a comfortable brain is a stagnant brain, and I think that’s a great mantra for anyone, regardless of age.

questions.question: David explains that the most important change in neuroscience is the understanding that
options: A) the brain's physical structure is determined at birth.
B) the brain remains adaptable throughout a person's life.
C) old age prevents the brain from learning new things.
D) hardware is more important than software in the brain.
questions.explanation: David states the discovery that the brain reorganizes itself 'well into old age' is the most profound shift.

questions.question: According to Claire, a student's motivation increases when they
options: A) realize that failing a math test is inevitable.
B) see their brain as a bucket that needs filling.
C) view learning difficulty as a biological growth process.
D) stop trying to build their mental 'muscles'.
questions.explanation: She tells students that 'struggle... is actually the sound of the brain growing new connections'.

questions.question: What was a surprising finding from the London taxi driver study?
options: A) Memorizing streets had no effect on the drivers' brains.
B) The brain's navigation area remained large even after retirement.
C) The physical changes in the brain were reversible.
D) The drivers were unable to memorize the city streets.
questions.explanation: David notes that when drivers retired, the brain area 'began to shrink back to its original size'.

questions.question: Claire’s main concern regarding digital technology is its effect on
options: A) the brain's ability to process short bursts of information.
B) the speed at which people can find answers online.
C) the capacity for people to concentrate for long periods.
D) the brain's physical ability to read digital clips.
questions.explanation: She is concerned that reliance on digital answers is 'weakening our capacity for deep, sustained focus'.

questions.question: David uses the example of the piano-playing seventy-year-olds to show that
options: A) music is the only way to improve brain function in the elderly.
B) older people are generally unable to change their cognitive habits.
C) cognitive flexibility can still be improved in later life.
D) learning a new hobby becomes impossible after sixty.
questions.explanation: The study showed that older adults learning piano had 'significant increases in cognitive flexibility'.

questions.question: What advice does Claire give for maintaining a healthy brain?
options: A) avoid tasks that make you feel uncomfortable
B) stick to hobbies that you are already good at
C) find activities that challenge your current abilities
D) maintain a stagnant but comfortable routine
questions.explanation: She advises seeking out things that make you feel like a beginner, as being 'out of your depth' is where growth happens.
